md: Scientific notation is the place where exponents repay their hard work. The mass of Earth is about $5{,}972{,}000{,}000{,}000{,}000{,}000{,}000{,}000$ kilograms. The radius of a hydrogen atom is about $0.0000000000529$ meters. Counting those zeros by hand is a nightmare; comparing them is worse. Scientific notation packs the same information into something you can read at a glance — and that you can multiply or divide using the exponent rules you already know.
### 1. The format
A number is in **scientific notation** when it's written as:

blocks.tex: a \times 10^n, \quad \text{where } 1 \le |a| < 10 \text{ and } n \text{ is an integer.}

md: The *coefficient* $a$ is a number with **exactly one nonzero digit before the decimal point**. The *exponent* $n$ tells you how many places the decimal must move to get the original number back.
Examples:

blocks.title: Why $1 \le |a| < 10$?
blocks.body: It gives every number a *unique* scientific-notation form. Without this rule, $45{,}000$ could be written as $4.5 \times 10^4$ or $45 \times 10^3$ or $0.45 \times 10^5$ — all equal, all named "scientific notation," all ambiguous. Fixing the coefficient to one digit before the decimal nails down a single canonical form.

md: ### 2. Converting from standard to scientific
Move the decimal point until you have **exactly one nonzero digit** before it. Then count the moves — that count becomes the exponent.
- If you moved the decimal **left**, the exponent is **positive** (the original number was big).
- If you moved the decimal **right**, the exponent is **negative** (the original number was small).

**Example A — big number.** $84{,}500$. Decimal starts at the end: $84500.$ Move it left until one nonzero digit remains in front: $8.4500$. That's 4 moves left, so the exponent is $+4$. Drop trailing zeros: $8.45 \times 10^4$.
**Example B — small number.** $0.00072$. Move the decimal right: $00007.2$ — that's 4 moves right, so the exponent is $-4$. Result: $7.2 \times 10^{-4}$.
### 3. Converting from scientific to standard
Read the exponent and move the decimal that many places — in the *opposite* direction from the conversion above.
- **Positive exponent** $\Rightarrow$ move the decimal **right** (number gets bigger).
- **Negative exponent** $\Rightarrow$ move the decimal **left** (number gets smaller).
**Example C.** $6.02 \times 10^5$. Move the decimal 5 places right, filling with zeros as needed: $602{,}000$.
**Example D.** $3.1 \times 10^{-3}$. Move the decimal 3 places left: $0.0031$.

blocks.title: Quick sanity check
blocks.body: Big positive exponent → big number. Big *negative* exponent → tiny number. If you converted and got the wrong size, you moved the decimal the wrong way.

md: ### 4. Multiplying and dividing in scientific notation

This is where exponent rules earn their keep. To multiply two numbers in scientific notation, multiply the coefficients and *add the exponents* (product rule). To divide, divide the coefficients and *subtract the exponents* (quotient rule).
**Example E.** $(3 \times 10^4)(2 \times 10^5) = 6 \times 10^{4 + 5} = 6 \times 10^9$.
**Example F.** $\dfrac{8 \times 10^7}{2 \times 10^3} = 4 \times 10^{7 - 3} = 4 \times 10^4$.
Sometimes the new coefficient lands outside the $1$-to-$10$ range. If that happens, slide a power of 10 in or out to re-normalize.

**Example G — re-normalize after multiplying.** $(5 \times 10^3)(4 \times 10^6) = 20 \times 10^9$. But $20$ isn't a valid coefficient (it's $\ge 10$). Rewrite: $20 = 2 \times 10^1$. So $20 \times 10^9 = 2 \times 10^{10}$.

**Example H — re-normalize after dividing.** $\dfrac{3 \times 10^4}{6 \times 10^2} = 0.5 \times 10^2$. But $0.5$ isn't valid ($< 1$). Rewrite: $0.5 = 5 \times 10^{-1}$. So $0.5 \times 10^2 = 5 \times 10^{1}$.
### 5. Numbers worth recognizing
| Quantity | Scientific notation |
| Speed of light | $3.0 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| Mass of Earth | $5.97 \times 10^{24}$ kg |
| Avogadro's number | $6.02 \times 10^{23}$ |
| Distance Earth to Sun | $1.5 \times 10^{11}$ m |
| Charge of an electron | $1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C |
| Mass of an electron | $9.1 \times 10^{-31}$ kg |
| Radius of a hydrogen atom | $5.3 \times 10^{-11}$ m |
These are the magnitudes that appear all over science. Reading them comfortably is a skill you'll use in every science class for the rest of school.

blocks.title: The power of scientific notation
blocks.body: How many times larger is the Sun-Earth distance than a hydrogen atom? In standard form, it'd be an arithmetic mess. In scientific notation: $\dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{11}}{5.3 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 0.28 \times 10^{22} \approx 3 \times 10^{21}$. Roughly $10^{21}$ atoms across — pretty much the whole calculation done by inspecting the exponents.
